coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and characterization of biomass CQDs doped WO2.72

coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Limin
Liu, Dongyue
Li, Xuejiao
Wu, Kejia
Zhang, Wanqi
coredata.dc:description: Biomass carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were widely used in bioimaging and WO2.72 has great potential in cancer therapy. The biomass CQDs were prepared by mixed acid oxidation method with biomass materials such as pods, tobacco leaves and cysteine as carbon sources. Properties of biomass carbon quantum dots were characterized, including micromorphology, the functional group composition, photoluminescence properties and fluorescence labeling ability in HepG2 cells. And the cytotoxicity of CQDs in HepG2 and MCF-7 cells was tested. In this paper, in situ loading of biomass CQDs on one dimensional long rod like WO2.72 (C-WO2.72) was synthesized by solvothermal method. Properties of C-WO2.72 were characterized, including the phase composition, the functional group composition, micromorphology and absorbability.

Tungsten oxide
Bioimaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images and particle size distribution diagram of CQDs. (a) pod, (b, c) tobacco leaf, (d, e) cysteine.
Fluorescence spectra of CQDs with different carbon sources. (a) pod, (b) tobacco leaf, (c) cysteine.
Laser confocal micrograph images of HepG2 cells labeled by CQDs. (a) 400 nm, (b) 488 nm, (c) 568 nm.
The survival rate curves of the HepG2 cell and MCF-7 cells.
TEM images of C-WO2.72. (a) Low ratio, (b, c) high ratio, (d) High resolution.
Comparison of lattice parameters and theoretical parameters of C-WO2.72.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and characterization of biomass CQDs doped WO2.72

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Harbin University of Science and Technology, Harbin, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering
Harbin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Engineering Dielectrics and Its Application, Ministry of Education, Harbin University of Science and Technology, Harbin, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Engineering Dielectrics and Its Application
Harbin University of Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Harbin University of Science and Technology, Harbin, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering
Harbin University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biomass carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were widely used in bioimaging and WO2.72 has great potential in cancer therapy. The biomass CQDs were prepared by mixed acid oxidation method with biomass materials such as pods, tobacco leaves and cysteine as carbon sources. Properties of biomass carbon quantum dots were characterized, including micromorphology, the functional group composition, photoluminescence properties and fluorescence labeling ability in HepG2 cells. And the cytotoxicity of CQDs in HepG2 and MCF-7 cells was tested. In this paper, in situ loading of biomass CQDs on one dimensional long rod like WO2.72 (C-WO2.72) was synthesized by solvothermal method. Properties of C-WO2.72 were characterized, including the phase composition, the functional group composition, micromorphology and absorbability.

ce:section.ce:para: CQDs [1–3] are a new development branch in the field of nanocarbon materials [4]. It has excellent physical and chemical properties such as fluorescence [5,6], upconversion luminescence [7], and biocompatibility [8]. Among the outstanding physical and chemical properties of CQDs, its optical properties are the most prominent characteristics, with the extensive value of research and broad application prospects [9,10]. The optical properties of CQDs include ultraviolet–visible absorption, photoluminescence [11], photostability [12], upconversion fluorescence emission, pH dependence [13] and Biocompatibility [14,15], which can be used for photoelectric conversion [16], photoelectrocatalysis [17], bioimaging [2], et al.

In recent years, biomass materials have been widely concerned as carbon sources for the preparation of CQDs [18,19]. Biomass materials have a wide range of sources, complex composition and excellent biocompatibility. Due to the complex composition of biomass materials, various doping elements can be provided for CQDs [20,21]. The application of biomass CQDs to cell fluorescence imaging has the following advantages. As one of the important elements constituting a living organism, carbon is excellent in biocompatibility. The CQDs have excellent fluorescence stability. CQDs have a small particle size, which is easily endocytosed by cells and can be excreted by organisms [22]. Researchers often use the advantages of CQDs to form a composite system with metal oxides or organometallic ligands [23,24].

At present, tungsten oxide is a research hotspot [25,26]. WO2.72 has a highly tunable structure and unique physicochemical properties that have potential for applications in photocatalysis [27], electrochemistry [28], Photothermal conversion [29] and photothermal therapy [30]. WO2.72 has applications in cancer treatment and biological tissue characterization.

In this work, Biomass CQDs were prepared by mixed acid oxidation method and characterized. We constructed the C-WO2.72 composite structure. The CQDs and WO2.72 were combined in situ. Biomass C-WO2.72 samples were characterized. The prepared C-WO2.72 system has potential application value in the fields of drug delivery, cell imaging and cancer therapy.

Tetramethylazozolium salt (MTT), Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), Trypsin were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. Dulbecco’s modified eagle medium (DMEM) was obtained from Gibico. Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was obtained from Hangzhou Sijiqing bioengineering Materials Co., Ltd. 4′,6-Diamidino-2-phenylindole dihydrochloride (DAPI) was obtained from Beijing Suo Laibao Technology Co., Ltd. Human breast cancer cell line (MCF-7), Human hepatoma cell (HepG2) was obtained from Chinese Academy of Sciences Shanghai Cell Bank. l-Cysteine was obtained from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute. Tungsten chloride (WCl6) was obtained from Shanghai Maclean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. N-propanol was obtained from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. Absolute ethanol was obtained from Tianjin Tianli Chemical Reagent Company. Sodium hydroxide was obtained from Tianjin Mainland Chemical Reagent Factory. Phosphate was obtained from Tianjin Yaohua Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Pod, Tobacco leaf, Deionized water were obtained from Harbin Yongchang Chemical Reagent Company. Concentrated sulfuric acid was purchased from Beijing chemical work. All the chemicals were used as received without further purification.

In this synthesis, biomass CQDs are prepared by mixed acid oxidation method. 3 g of cysteine was placed in a porcelain boat and roasted at 150 °C for 2 h in an air atmosphere, followed by grinding. The pods and tobacco leaves were dried at 60 °C and then pulverized. 0.5 g of the treated biomass material was placed in a three-necked bottle of concentrated sulfuric acid 10 mL and phosphoric acid 5 mL, and stirred at 140 °C for 12 h in an oil bath until the sample was carbonized to a black solid. After the reaction, the sample was diluted with distilled water and poured into a beaker. NaOH was added until the pH of the mixed solution was near neutral. The mixture was filtered through a 60 mL funnel, and the obtained solid was placed in an oven at 80 °C for 2 h, and triturated to obtain a black powder. CQDs were obtained.

MCF-7 and HepG2 cells were cultured in DMEM containing penicillin (100 U mL−1), streptomycin (100 U mL−1), and 10% FBS in a humidified incubator at 37 °C and 5% CO2. Purified cells were harvested by PBS flushing fluid and trypsin. MCF-7 cells were cultured in 96-well plates as a density of 6 × 104 cells/mL. Solutions containing CQDs with different concentrations was added to the culture medium. After incubation for 48 h, 96-well plates were washed twice with PBS, and 180 μL of medium and 20 μL of LTTT were added. After incubation for 4 h, 150 μL of DMSO were added. The detection was carried out using a microplate reader, and the wavelength was selected to be 496 nm. The experimental data were analyzed by SPSS 19.0 software. HepG2 cells at a concentration of 1 × 105 cells/mL were seeded into 6-well plates. 1 mL of medium was added to a 6-well plate. The cells were cultured overnight and then a CQDs solution was added. After 2 h, the CQDs solution was aspirated and the cells were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 10 min. Rinse three times with PBS and add DAPI for 2–3 min. Rinse three times with PBS and remove the cover slip to dry. Place a small drop of the mounting medium on the slide and place the cover slip on the slide. HepG2 cells were stored in the absence of light for 24 h, and the phagocytosis of CQDs was observed by laser confocal microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of biomass CQDs doped WO2.72
ce:section.ce:para: 0.1983 g of WCl6, 0.099 g of CQDs and 100 mL of n-propanol were placed in the reaction vessel. The mixture was incubated at 180 °C for 12 h and then cooled to room temperature. The dark blue liquid was taken out from the reaction vessel and centrifuged at 9000 r/min for 7 min. The supernatant was washed with absolute ethanol and centrifuged again. The solid sample was washed twice with absolute ethanol and then placed in a drying oven at 80 °C for 2 h. Blue-green powder samples were obtained.

Crystal structures and phase purity of the samples were determined using a D8 Advance powder X-ray diffractometer (XRD) with Cu Kɑ radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) with an operation voltage and current maintained at 40 kV and 30 mA. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded using a JEM-2100 TEM system operating at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. Analyze the functional groups and characteristic chemical bonds of the material to be tested. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was recorded using a Nicolet iS10 FTIR Spectrometer from Thermo Fisher Scientific. Fluorescence spectroscopy was recorded using RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer with a wavelength range of 350–650 nm. UV–visible-near infrared spectroscopy was recorded using UV757CRT UV spectrophotometer manufactured by Shanghai Guangguang Co., Ltd. The scanning wavelength range is 200–1000 nm. The absorbance was measured in the cytotoxicity experiment using the 680 type enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay from Bio-Rad. Cell staining was performed using an Olympus LSM510META confocal laser scanning biomicroscope. All the spectral measurements were performed at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functional groups and characteristic chemical bond analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the FT-IR of biomass CQDs prepared by mixing acid oxidation method. There is a distinct –OH extension vibrational peak at 3430 cm−1 and an absorption peak of –CH3 at 1385 cm−1. The CQDs produced by tobacco leaves contain N functional groups (–NN, 1592 cm−1) and S functional groups (SO, 1033 cm−1). The CQDs produced by the pods contain N (–NN, 1628 cm−1) functional groups, S (S = 1112 cm−1) functional groups, and C–Cl (618 cm−1) functional groups.

The presence of multiple elemental functional groups in CQDs is due to the complex composition of tobacco and pods. The main components of tobacco include carbohydrates, nitrogenous compounds (such as proteins, nicotine and free base), organic acids and minerals. The main components of pods include lipids, alcohols, and ketones. Biomass CQDs contains hydrophilic groups, hydroxyl groups and sulfonic acid groups. It is indicated that the prepared biomass CQDs have good hydrophilicity.

Fig. 2 shows the TEM image and particle size distribution image of biomass CQDs prepared by pods, tobacco leaves and cysteine. The TEM image shows that the morphology of CQDs is approximately spherical and the particle size is about 1–5 nm. The average particle diameter of the carbon quantum dots prepared by the tobacco leaves was 2.34 nm, and the data was shown as Fig. 2(c). Prepared CQDs have good dispersibility and no obvious agglomeration. In Fig. 2(b), the CQDs have face spacing of about 0.206 nm. Fig. 2(d, e) show that the CQDs at the edge of the reactants begins to fall off when the strong acid oxidizes the reaction material.

The CQDs of different carbon sources are tested with different excitation wavelengths, and the obtained CQDs fluorescence emission lines are shown in Fig. 3 . The luminescence wavelength of CQDs varies with the excitation wavelength, which exhibits the characteristics of upconversion luminescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging of CQDs in cells
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 is a luminescence photograph of carbon quantum dot-labeled HepG2 cells irradiated with laser light at different excitation wavelengths. The concentration of the CQDs aqueous solution of the cell culture was 0.5 mg/mL. HepG2 cells phagocytosis CQDs during cell culture successfully by laser confocal microscopy. CQDs cells show excellent photoluminescence properties and excitation wavelength dependence in HepG2 cells. The intracellular CQDs showed blue luminescence at a wavelength of 400 nm, green luminescence at a wavelength of 488 nm, and red luminescence at a wavelength of 568 nm, which were shown in Fig. 4(a–c), respectively.

In Fig. 4, the bright blue part of the cell is the nucleus labeled by DAPI staining, and the small fluorescent particles in the cytoplasm are CQDs that the cells phagocytose. Because of the size of the quantum dots is about 5 nm, it can enter the nucleus through the exchange of substances between the nucleus and the cytoplasm, The CQDs are dispersed in the whole cell to achieve the purpose of cell optical labeling. CQDs can be used to distinguish different cell tissue by the specific dye.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CQDs on cell survival rate
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 is a graph showing the effect of CQDs on the survival rate of HepG2 cells and MCF-7 cells. The cell survival rate showed a gradual decline with the increase of CQDs concentration, and it was found that CQDs showed no significant biotoxicity in the low concentration range. According to statistics, the survival rate of the two test cells can reach more than 80% when the concentration of the aqueous solution of CQDs is less than 0.5 mg/mL, and the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs also meets the observation requirements.

CQDs are mainly composed of carbon elements. As one of the important elements that make up living organisms, carbon itself is not toxic. Thus CQDs exhibit very low biotoxicity at lower concentrations. The small size effect and interfacial effect exhibited by the small particle size of the CQDs make it highly active. When the CQDs are excessively accumulated in the cells (the concentration of the CQDs solution is more than 0.5 mg/mL), the CQDs exhibit a significant increase in cytotoxicity. The survival rate of HepG2 cells and MCF-7 cells was about 46% when the concentration reached 2.0 mg/mL, which was consistent with the phenomenon observed by other researchers. The reason may be that an excessively high content of CQDs destroys the ionic balance system of the intracellular cytoplasm, leading to apoptosis of the cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phase composition and characteristic chemical bond analysis of C-WO2.72
ce:section.ce:para: The crystal phase of the as-prepared C-WO2.72 sample was investigated by XRD, as showed in Fig. 6 . The C-WO2.72 phase is a two phase mixtures. The main crystalline phase is WO2.72 (5–392), and the secondary crystalline phase is WO3 (54–508) with a diffraction peak at 2θ = 25.9°. The two peak positions of the (5 0 2) and (0 2 0) crystal faces of the WO2.72 crystal phase are shifted to some extent than the diffraction peak positions of the standard card (5–392). This may be due to the fact that C in the reaction forms a solid solution with the formation phase WO2.72, resulting in a change in the interplanar spacing. It may be that C forms a solid solution with WO2.72, which changes the interplanar spacing.

We calculated the unit cell parameters of the sample to investigate the relationship between the change in the interplanar spacing of WO2.72 and the change in unit cell parameters. The data in Table 1 show that the unit cell size of the C-WO2.72 experimental sample is slightly larger than the theoretical value, which may be due to the doping of C or the existence of large residual stress in the generated unit cell. The lattice parameters of C-WO2.72 are shown in Table 1 by XRD line fitting calculation.

Fig. 7 is an infrared spectrum of C-WO2.72. As showed in Fig. 7, functional groups and chemical bonds such as O–H, –CC–, –CH3, C–O exist in the surface and structure of C-WO2.72. The vibration absorption peak at 827 cm-1 is the vibration absorption peak of the C-O-W bond, indicating that the C atom in the CQDs forms a new chemical bond with the W-O bond in WO2.72 during the synthesis.

Fig. 8 is the TEM image of C-WO2.72. C-WO2.72 was prepared by solvothermal, which are a nanorod having a radial dimension of about 20 nm and an axial dimension of about 300 nm in Fig. 8(b). Fig. 8(d) shows that the C-WO2.72 nanorods have an interplanar spacing of 3.7789 Å, corresponding to a (0 1 0) crystal plane of WO2.72. The WO2.72 nanorod may be the result of selective growth of the crystal along the (0 1 0) crystal plane, which makes the C-WO2.72 larger specific surface area.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of light absorption capacity of C-WO2.72
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9 is a UV–visible-near-infrared absorption spectrum of CQDs doped WO2.72. C-WO2.72 has obvious absorption in the near-ultraviolet, visible and near-infrared regions and significant absorption peaks at 260 nm, 530 nm and 760 nm. The absorption peak at 260 nm is caused by the π–π* transition of CC of the CQDs. The absorption peaks at 530 nm and 760 nm are derived from the oxygen vacancy defects in WO2.72. C-WO2.72 has a broad absorption capacity in the 260 nm to 1000 nm wavelength ranges. Therefore, C-WO2.72 has potential for application in PTT and PDT.

In summary, biomass CQDs are synthesized by mixing acid oxidation method. The CQDs have a size of 2–5 nm, a nearly spherical morphology, good dispersibility. The biomass CQDs has excitation wavelength dependence and upconversion luminescence characteristics, and its luminescent color is concentrated in the blue-green region. CQDs can be endocytosed by HepG2 cells and have a good distribution in HepG2 cells. It can still exhibit good photoluminescence properties in cells, enabling multicolor luminescence within cells. The CQDs tested by the MTT method showed low biotoxicity to both HepG2 cells and MCF-7 cells. When the concentration of CQDs aqueous solution was 0.5 mg/mL, the survival rate of HepG2 cells was 87.71%, and the survival rate of MCF-7 cells was 82.12%.

A one-dimensional rod-shaped C-WO2.72 nanomaterial was obtained by in-situ composite of CQDs with WO2.72. C-WO2.72 has strong absorption capacity in the ultraviolet region (260 nm), the visible region (530 nm), and the near-infrared region (760 nm). C-WO2.72 has great potential to build an integrated platform for bioimaging and cancer therapy.
